coredata.dc:title: A facile ratiometric sensing platform based on inner filter effect for hypochlorous acid detection
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Chenggong
Zhou, Yanmei
Zhou, Yunhao
Li, Zhaoge
Peng, Xiaojun
coredata.dc:description: The easily constructed fluorescent probes with excellent sensing performance have attracted increasing attention in recent years. In this work, by virtue of the contrasting light absorption properties of neutral red (NR) and its product NR-NO after the reaction with hypochlorous acid (HOCl) for the fluorescence released from 4-triphenylvinyl-biphenyl (TPEP)-based nanoparticles (T-NPs), a facile inner filter effect (IFE)-based ratiometric sensing platform (SP) was constructed. Upon the addition of HOCl, a typical ratiometric fluorescence change and weak cross-talk between the emission bands (128 nm ≤ Δλem ≤ 182 nm) can be observed from SP, which effectively avoids the spectral overlap and ensures the high sensitivity (LOD = 62 nM) and accuracy in HOCl detection. In addition, because of the good water dispersibility and solubility featured in T-NPs and NR respectively, the SP can also be successfully applied to HOCl detection in 100% aqueous solution and live cell imaging. Moreover, the ratiometric SP avoids the complex covalent linking between fluorophore and energy acceptor, thereby providing great flexibility and convenience for its construction and application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: HOCl detection
Ratiometric sensing platform
Inner filter effect
Weak cross-talk
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: HOCl detection in real water samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of SP for HOCl detection based on IFE.
Synthetic procedures of TPEP and T-NPs.

(a) The proposed reaction mechanism of NR towards HOCl. (b) Fluorescence spectra of T-NPs (5 μg mL−1) and UV–vis absorption spectra of NR (4 μM) in the absence and presence of HOCl (50 μM). (c) Fluorescence lifetimes of T-NPs (5 μg mL−1) in the absence and presence of NR (4 μM), as well as HOCl (50 μM).
(a) Fluorescence spectra of NR (4 μM) in the absence and presence of HOCl (50 μM). (b) Time-course of fluorescence intensity at 644 nm of NR (4 μM) with HOCl (50 μM). λex =538 nm.

(a) Fluorescence intensity changes of TPEP (10 μM) at 487 nm in DMSO with increasing water volume content. Inset: Fluorescence (left) and Tyndall effect (right) of TPEP in DMSO and DMSO-water solution (1: 99 v/v). (b) The normalized fluorescence spectra of TPEP and absorption spectra of NR. (c) SEM images of TPEP and (d) T-NPs. Inset: Size distributions of TPEP and T-NPs determined by DLS.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of T-NPs (5 μg mL−1) in the presence of different concentrations of NR. λex =331 nm. (b) Fluorescence quenching efficiency of T-NPs (5 μg mL−1) versus concentrations of NR. (c) Fluorescence lifetimes of T-NPs (5 μg mL−1) in the absence and presence of NR (4 μM).

(a) Fluorescence intensity ratio (F516/F644) changes of SP in the absence and presence of HOCl (50 μM) at various pH values. (b) Fluorescence spectra and (c) fluorescence intensity ratio (F516/F644) of SP in the presence of HOCl (50 μM) and relevant analytes (500 μM). (d) Fluorescence intensity ratio (F516/F644) of SP towards HOCl (50 μM) in the presence of other potential interferences (500 μM). (1) blank, (2) HOCl, (3) H2O2, (4) ONOO−, (5)
OH, (6) 1O2, (7) O2 −, (8) Cys, (9) Hcy, (10) GSH, (11) Na+, (12) K+, (13) Fe2+, (14) Al3+, (15) Mg2+, (16) Zn2+, (17) Cl−, (18) F−, (19) CH3COO−, (20) SO3 2-, (21) HCO3 −.

(a) Absorption and (b) fluorescence spectra of SP in the presence of different concentrations of HOCl. (c) Linear relationship between fluorescence intensity ratio (F516/F644) of SP versus HOCl concentration. (d) Photographs of SP in the absence and presence of HOCl (50 μM) under daylight and 365 nm UV light.

Fluorescence imaging for PC12 cells pretreated with (a) 0 μM, (b) 10 μM, (c) 20 μM, (d) 30 μM and (e) 50 μM HOCl for 30 min and then incubated with SP. Blue channel: λex =405 nm, collected at 420-580 nm. Red channel: λex =488 nm, collected at 610-720 nm. Scale bar: 20 μm (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Comparison of the reported fluorescent probes for HOCl.
Results for the determination of HOCl in tap water and lake water.

head.ce:title: A facile ratiometric sensing platform based on inner filter effect for hypochlorous acid detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University, Kaifeng, 475004, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Henan Joint International Research Laboratory of Environmental Pollution Control Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University, Kaifeng, 475004, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian, 116024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals, Dalian University of Technology, Dalian, 116024, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The easily constructed fluorescent probes with excellent sensing performance have attracted increasing attention in recent years. In this work, by virtue of the contrasting light absorption properties of neutral red (NR) and its product NR-NO after the reaction with hypochlorous acid (HOCl) for the fluorescence released from 4-triphenylvinyl-biphenyl (TPEP)-based nanoparticles (T-NPs), a facile inner filter effect (IFE)-based ratiometric sensing platform (SP) was constructed. Upon the addition of HOCl, a typical ratiometric fluorescence change and weak cross-talk between the emission bands (128 nm ≤ Δλem ≤ 182 nm) can be observed from SP, which effectively avoids the spectral overlap and ensures the high sensitivity (LOD = 62 nM) and accuracy in HOCl detection. In addition, because of the good water dispersibility and solubility featured in T-NPs and NR respectively, the SP can also be successfully applied to HOCl detection in 100% aqueous solution and live cell imaging. Moreover, the ratiometric SP avoids the complex covalent linking between fluorophore and energy acceptor, thereby providing great flexibility and convenience for its construction and application.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence imaging has emerged as an attractive technique because of its simplicity, high sensitivity and non-invasive visualization capability, and a large number of fluorescent probes were proposed in recent years for the sensing and imaging of HOCl due to its vital physiological functions in living cells [1–8]. Among them, ratiometric fluorescent probes tend to exhibit higher accuracy than “turn-on/off” types by virtue of its two separate emission bands that can efficiently overcome the interferences caused by environmental factors, such as polarity, probe concentration, temperature, laser power, etc [9–14]. Nevertheless, most of them are mainly constructed according to the intermolecular charge transfer (ICT) and Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanisms, which often need for complex molecular design and organic synthesis or even complicated covalent linking to ensure a suitable distance between fluorophore and energy acceptor [15–19]. Therefore, their wider application in the field of sensing is restricted to a certain extent.

Encouragingly, in an inner filter effect (IFE)-based detection system, the excitation or emission light can be absorbed by absorbers when the absorption spectra of absorbers overlaps with the fluorescence excitation or emission spectra of fluorophores, which makes the absorption signal can be successfully converted into a more pronounced fluorescence signal, and then endow it with much higher sensitivity [20–25]. Moreover, there is no need for any covalent linking between fluorophore and absorber, which makes it more flexible and convenient in probe construction [26–33]. Therefore, some IFE-based probes were proposed in recent years with the help of various fluorophores, such as carbon dots (CDs), quantum dots (QDs), gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [34–39]. However, to the best of our knowledge, most of them only showed a single-channel fluorescence response upon the addition of corresponding analytes, and the IFE-based ratiometric fluorescent probes with dual-channel fluorescence response in HOCl detection have never been reported. Hence, it is of great interest and importance to propose some facile but high-performance fluorescent probes for HOCl detection.

Herein, considering the excellent aggregation-induced emission (AIE) properties of 4-triphenylvinyl-biphenyl (TPEP), the hexadecyl trimethyl ammonium bromide (CTAB)-coated TPEP nanoparticles (T-NPs) with good water dispersibility were fabricated and used as fluorophores. Then, by mixing T-NPs with water soluble neutral red (NR), a facile IFE-based HOCl sensing platform (SP) was constructed. As shown in Scheme 1 , by virtue of the strong IFE between T-NPs and NR, the NR with red emission can also serve as the light absorber and efficiently absorb the fluorescence released from T-NPs, especially the fluorescence at longer wavelength, which decreased and blue-shifted the fluorescence obtained from T-NPs. Then, upon the addition of HOCl, the NR can be oxidized into NR-NO immediately, accompanied by the quenching of its red fluorescence. In addition, the IFE between T-NPs and NR-NO is relatively weak and NR-NO is more inclined to absorb the fluorescence at shorter wavelength released from T-NPs. In consequence, the decreased fluorescence of T-NPs can be recovered and an obvious red shift was observed in its fluorescence emission. Finally, the SP was successfully applied to HOCl detection in real water samples and live cell imaging, and exhibited excellent sensing performance.

All chemicals and reagents were purchased from commercial suppliers and used without further purification. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was obtained from a Millipore water purification system and used throughout the experiments. The MS spectra and NMR spectra were obtained from Bruker AmaZon SL and Bruker DMX-400 respectively. The UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra were acquired from U-4100 spectrophotometer and Edinburgh FS5 spectrofluorometer respectively. The SEM and TEM images were obtained from JSM-7610 F and JEM-2100 respectively. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiments were carried out using Zetasizer Nano ZS. The cell imaging was performed with a confocal laser scanning microscope (Nikon/A1+N-SIM).

The synthetic procedures of TPEP and T-NPs were shown in Scheme 2 . TPEP was synthesized according to the reported literatures [40,41]. 2-bromo-1,1,2-triphenylethylene (1 g, 3.0 mmol) and 4-biphenyl boronic acid (0.59 g, 3.0 mmol) were dissolved into 50 mL of ethanol in a 100 mL round bottom flask, and then 4 mL of potassium carbonate solution (2 mol/L) was added. Then, the mixture was degassed with nitrogen for 10 min and then Pd(Pph3)4 (10 mg, 8.7 μmol) was added and heated to reflux for 8 h. After that, the organic layer was dried over anhydrous sodium sulfate, and the solvent was removed under reduced pressure. Finally, the residue was purified by silica column chromatography using petroleum ether: methylene chloride (1: 1 v/v) as eluent, and a white powder was obtained in 59% yield. 1H NMR (400 MHz, DMSO-d6) δ 7.66−7.57 (m, 2 H), 7.52−7.38 (m, 4 H), 7.37−7.28 (m, 1 H), 7.24−7.08 (m, 9 H), 7.08−6.93 (m, 8 H). 13C NMR (101 MHz, Chloroform-d6) δ 143.73, 142.76, 140.54, 131.77, 131.42, 131.35, 128.68, 127.75, 127.69, 127.64, 127.15, 126.85, 126.48, 126.42, 126.20, 53.43. APCI-MS: m/z, calcd: 408.19, found: 408.97 ([M + H]+). Then, the TPEP molecules were dissolved in tetrahydrofuran (THF) to make a THF solution with the concentration of 1 mM. Afterwards, 6 mL of the TPEP solution was added to 60 mL of PBS buffer solution containing 1 mM of CTAB, and the mixture was stirred vigorously at room temperature for 24 h. Finally, after the dialysis against MilliQ water and drying in vacuum, the T-NPs were successfully obtained.

After the synthesis of TPEP and T-NPs, the TPEP (10 μM) in 10 mM PBS buffer-DMSO solution (99: 1 v/v) and T-NPs (5 μg mL−1) in 10 mM PBS buffer solution were carefully dropped onto a clean silicon wafer, and then dried in vacuum. After that, the samples were sprayed with gold, and the SEM images for TPEP and T-NPs were obtained at a magnification of 2000× and 60,000× respectively.

The stock solutions of T-NPs (200 mg L−1), NR and various analytes (1 mM) were prepared in ultrapure water respectively. All the detection experiments were carried out within 10 mM PBS buffer (pH = 6.5) at room temperature. The HOCl detection in tap water and lake water was performed within a 3 mL detection system after filtration.

PC12 cells were cultured in Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM, pH = 7.4) in the atmosphere of 5% CO2 at 37 ℃. The confocal images were obtained from a confocal laser scanning microscope. The emission of the blue channel was collected in the range of 420−580 nm under 405 nm excitation, and the red channel was collected in the range of 610−720 nm under 488 nm excitation.

According to the reports that aniline compounds can be oxidized into nitroso compounds by HOCl, the water soluble and commercially available NR with an amino group on its benzene ring was selected and its fluorescent performance was studied for the first time upon the addition of HOCl [42–44]. As shown in Fig. 1 , NR can exhibit a red fluorescence centered at 644 nm under 538 nm excitation. Then, upon the addition of HOCl, an obvious fluorescence decrease was observed from NR, and the time-course experiment showed that the reaction can be completed in about 5 s, which indicated NR might serve as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe for the rapid recognition of HOCl.

Considering the ratiometric fluorescent probes feature with higher accuracy than “turn-off” types, we tried to introduce another fluorogens directly to construct an IFE-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for HOCl detection. Hence, fluorogens with AIE characteristics (AIEgens) gradually came into our mind because of its high brightness, good photostability, large stokes shift and the excellent anti-aggregation-caused quenching (anti-ACQ) capacity [45]. Among various AIEgens, TPEP was selected due to its easy synthesis as well as its emission spectra overlaps well with the UV–vis absorption spectra of NR shown in Fig. 2 a and Fig. 2b. Moreover, in order to take advantage of the AIE characteristics of TPEP and endow it with good water dispersibility, the T-NPs were fabricated by encapsulating TPEP with CTAB. As can be seen from Fig. 2c and Fig. 2d, the TPEP without encapsulation by CTAB only showed an irregular morphology. However, after the encapsulation, the formed T-NPs exhibited uniform spheres with an average particle size of about 82 nm, which indicated that our T-NPs were successfully synthesized.

By virtue of the good overlap between the UV–vis absorption spectra of NR and the fluorescence spectra of TPEP, the fluorescent performance of T-NPs in the presence of different concentrations of NR was studied. As can be seen from Fig. 3 a and Fig. 3b, upon the addition of NR from 0 to 4 μM, an obvious fluorescence decrease and blue shift from 487 nm to 462 nm was observed from T-NPs, and the fluorescence quenching efficiency was up to 83%. Then, the fluorescence lifetime experiment was carried out. As shown in Fig. 3c, after the addition of NR, the fluorescence lifetime of T-NPs remained almost unchanged, which indicated the fluorescence decrease of T-NPs, as well as its fluorescence blue shift can be attributed to the IFE between T-NPs and NR [28,46]. Moreover, considering the good fluorescence quenching phenomenon of NR in the presence of HOCl, the system containing 5 μg mL−1 T-NPs and 4 μM NR was selected as the SP, and its photophysical properties were further studied.

In order to determine the optimal working conditions of SP, the effect of pH on its sensing performance for HOCl was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 a, upon the addition of HOCl, a significant fluorescence intensity ratio (F516/F644) change was observed, and the maximum value was located at around pH = 6.5. At the same time, the SP itself showed only a low fluorescence intensity ratio. Hence, all the subsequent spectral experiments were performed in a PBS buffer solution at pH = 6.5.

The selectivity of SP towards HOCl over other potential interferences including H2O2, ONOO−,
OH, 1O2, O2 −, Cys, Hcy, GSH, Na+, K+, Fe2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cl−, F−, CH3COO−, SO3 2- and HCO3 − was studied. As shown in Fig. 4b and c, only HOCl can give significant changes in the emission spectra and fluorescence intensity ratio (F516/F644) featured in SP, indicating the SP has good selectivity for HOCl. In addition, the anti-interference capability was further studied. As can be seen from Fig. 4d, in the detection of HOCl, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F516/F644) only fluctuates slightly in the presence of other interferences. Hence, all the results showed that the SP featured with good selectivity and anti-interference capability in HOCl detection.

The sensing performance of SP for HOCl was studied in detail. As can be seen from Fig. 5 a, with the addition of increasing concentrations of HOCl from 0 to 50 μM, the absorption peak at 528 nm decreased, accompanied by a slow increase at 441 nm, which is consistent with the colorimetric change from red to yellow shown in Fig. 5d. As for the emission spectra shown in Fig. 5b, an apparent fluorescence decrease centered at 644 nm was observed, accompanied by a significant fluorescence increase and red shift centered from 462 nm to 516 nm, which is also consistent with the fluorescence colour change from weak-blue to strong-green under 365 nm UV light irradiation shown in Fig. 5d. In addition, the weak cross-talk between the emission bands (128 nm ≤ Δλem ≤ 182 nm) from SP can effectively avoid the spectral overlap and then ensure the high sensitivity and accuracy in HOCl detection [47–50]. Then, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F516/F644) was carefully plotted versus the HOCl concentration. As shown in Fig. 5c, a good linear relationship was observed in the HOCl concentration range from 0 to 24 μM and the detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 62 nM according to 3σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank samples measured 11 times and k is the slope obtained from the calibration curve [51,52]. Therefore, the results above clearly indicated that the SP can be successfully applied to HOCl detection quantitatively and exhibited high sensitivity. Moreover, compared with the fluorescent probes shown in Table 1 , our SP can exhibit some better characteristics in HOCl detection.

According to the previously reported literatures, we speculate the amino group on NR can be oxidized into nitroso by HOCl, and the proposed reaction mechanism of NR towards HOCl was shown in Scheme 3 a. The reaction mixture of NR with HOCl was analyzed by MS. As shown in Fig. S1 in the Supporting Information, the mass peak ([Reaction product]+ found m/z 266.96) accord with NR-NO was obtained from the reaction mixture. In addition, the peak of NR itself ([Neutral Red]+ calcd m/z 253.14) still exists. Hence, the result indicated NR-NO is the product of NR after the reaction with HOCl. Then, in order to further explore the fluorescence change mechanism of T-NPs in the presence of NR and HOCl, the spectral and fluorescence lifetime experiments were carried out. As can be seen from Scheme 3b, Scheme 3c and Fig. S2 in the Supporting Information, after the addition of HOCl, a significant decrease and blue shift of UV–vis absorption spectra was observed from NR, and the fluorescence quenching efficiency obtained from T-NPs decreased from 83% to 64%. In addition, the almost constant fluorescence lifetime also indicated that there is a weak IFE between T-NPs and NR-NO. Consequently, the obvious fluorescence increase and red shift observed from T-NPs from 462 nm to 516 nm can be attributed to the difference in the light absorption properties of NR and NR-NO for the fluorescence released from T-NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HOCl detection in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Because of the good water dispersibility and solubility featured in T-NPs and NR respectively, we tried to apply SP to HOCl detection in 100% aqueous solution, and the tap water and lake water were obtained from Henan University for further study. As can be seen from Fig. S3, after the addition of tap water, an evident UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra changes can be observed from SP. Then, according to the calibration curve obtained from Fig. 5c, the actual HOCl concentration in tap water was calculated to be 10.18 μM, which is consistent with the recommended HOCl concentration of about 8.4 μM [53,54]. Then, in order to further verify the accuracy of our SP in HOCl detection, the recovery experiments were carried out. As shown in Table 2 , the recovery of known amounts of HOCl added in tap water and lake water were achieved from 97.0%–106.5%, which indicated our SP can be efficiently applied to the accurate detection of HOCl in real water samples.

In order to study the sensing performance of SP for HOCl in living cells, the confocal cell imaging experiment was performed. After incubation of PC12 cells with (a) 0 μM HOCl, (b) 10 μM HOCl, (c) 20 μM HOCl, (d) 30 μM HOCl and (e) 50 μM HOCl for 30 min, and then incubated with SP for 30 min, the confocal images were collected in blue and red channel respectively. As shown in Fig. 6 , with the HOCl concentration increase, a noticeable fluorescence enhancement was observed from PC12 cells in the blue channel, accompanied by a decreased fluorescence in the red channel, which signaled that the SP can be successfully used for HOCl imaging in intricate living cells.

In summary, we proposed the first IFE-based ratiometric SP for HOCl detection. By virtue of the good water dispersibility and solubility featured in T-NPs and NR respectively, the SP was successfully applied to HOCl detection in environmental water samples and live cell imaging. In addition, the weak cross-talk between the emission bands can effectively avoid the spectral overlap and then ensure the high sensitivity and accuracy in HOCl detection. Most importantly, the construction and application of SP is pretty flexible and convenient, so we believe the SP can be successfully applied to many fields in the future.

Chenggong Xu: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing - original draft. Yanmei Zhou: Supervision, Project administration. Yunhao Zhou: Investigation. Zhaoge Li: Formal analysis. Xiaojun Peng: Validation, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21978069, 21776061), the State Key Laboratory of Fine Chemicals (KF1817), the program for Science & Technology Innovation Team in Universities of Henan Province (19IRTSTHN029).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2020.128766.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

J. Am. Chem. Soc.

Zhang, Real-Time Discrimination and Versatile Profiling of Spontaneous Reactive Oxygen Species in Living Organisms with a Single Fluorescent Probe, J Am Chem Soc, 138 (2016) 3769-3778.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric and colorimetric fluorescent probe for hypochlorite monitor and application for bioimaging in living cells, bacteria and zebrafish
J. Hazard. Mater.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chenggong Xu received his M.S. degree in biosensor from University of Jinan in 2017. He is a doctor candidate in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Henan University now. His current research interest involves the synthesis and application of fluorescent probe.

Yanmei Zhou received her Ph.D. degree in environmental engineering from Beijing University of Technology in 2006. She is working in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Henan University as a professor now. Her current interests include fluorescent probes, supercapacitor and hydrolysis of cellulose.

Yunhao Zhou is currently a master candidate in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Henan University. His current research interest involves the synthesis and application of fluorescent carbon dots.

Zhaoge Li is currently a master candidate in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering of Henan University. Her current research interest involves the synthesis and application of fluorescent probe.

Xiaojun Peng received his Ph.D. degree from Dalian University of Technology in 1986. He is working in State Key Laboratory, Fine Chemicals of Dalian University of Technology as a professor now. His current interests include fluorescent probe and functional dye.
